case_name: D.Y. Ex Rel. DeShawn Y. v. Catskill Regional Medical Center

summary.brief: D.Y. Ex Rel. DeShawn Y. v. Catskill Regional Medical Center. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: On October 18, 2011, defendant Amarjit Gill (hereinafter defendant), a pediatrician, examined his nine-year-old patient, plaintiff D.Y. (hereinafter the child), in connection with complaints of vomiting, diarrhea, the presence of blood in his urine and his inability to keep fluids down over a three-day period.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Defendant diagnosed the child with acute gastroenteritis and severe dehydration, ordered a stool study and requested a surgical consult.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The CT scan revealed a large abscess in the child’s pelvis caused by a ruptured appendix.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiffs’ expert testified that the child’s medical records reflected that he repeatedly complained of abdominal pain over the period of October 18, 2011 through October 21, 2011, and that the child had an increasingly elevated and abnormal white blood cell band count.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiffs' expert

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, we find that Supreme Court properly denied defendant’s CPLR 4401 motion for a directed verdict.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to plaintiffs, a juror could rationally conclude that defendant’s failure to expand his diagnosis and order a CT scan on or before October 21, 2011 caused the child’s underlying condition to remain undetected and unnecessarily worsen.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: Vomiting, diarrhea, blood in urine, inability to keep fluids down
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Symptoms present for three days, severe dehydration noted

focused_exam.abdomen: Abdominal pain noted, profuse watery diarrhea

requestables.name: Stool Study
requestables.description: Stool study to check for infections

reveal.decision_impact: Led to treatment for Clostridium difficile infection

requestables.name: CT Scan of Abdomen and Pelvis
requestables.description: CT scan to evaluate abdominal pain

reveal.result_summary: Large abscess in pelvis due to ruptured appendix

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Child presents with vomiting, diarrhea, and dehydration

timeline_phases.name: Hospital Admission and Workup
timeline_phases.description: Child admitted to hospital, initial diagnosis and treatment

decision_points.clinical_context: Child with persistent abdominal pain and elevated white blood cell count
decision_points.prompt: Should a CT scan be ordered to investigate the cause of abdominal pain?

options.description: Order CT scan of abdomen and pelvis
options.clinical_reasoning: Necessary to rule out appendicitis or other serious conditions
Assumed gastroenteritis and treated for Clostridium difficile

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not order CT scan until October 24, 2011
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Focused on treating for Clostridium difficile infection

expected_action_court.description: CT scan should have been ordered earlier
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated earlier CT scan was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order CT scan led to delayed diagnosis of ruptured appendix
explanation.what_should_have_happened: CT scan should have been ordered by October 21, 2011
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Child developed a large abscess due to ruptured appendix
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Required surgical intervention and prolonged antibiotic treatment

key_findings: Failure to order CT scan earlier was not proven to be a substantial factor in causing injury

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to order CT imaging earlier
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Earlier CT could have identified appendicitis sooner

factual_timeline.event: Child examined by pediatrician for vomiting and diarrhea

allegations.allegation: Failure to order CT scan earlier

court_findings.finding: No liability found for delayed CT scan
